scenario: A team is choosing between gRPC and REST for a new integration. They want broad compatibility -- the integration should work from Java, Python, browsers, mobile apps.
question: Which API style fits the broadest compatibility requirement?

options.text: gRPC is the universally compatible protocol for Camunda 8 API clients because it provides better performance and works across all programming languages and runtime environments including web browsers -- this description does not reflect actual behaviour for the requirement. While gRPC provides excellent performance and type safety, it has narrower runtime support than REST. gRPC-Web support in browsers requires a proxy layer and has additional complexity. Many scripting environments, serverless platforms, and lightweight runtimes lack gRPC client library support. For maximum cross-platform compatibility, REST is the appropriate choice.

REST and gRPC are equally compatible across all programming languages, runtimes, and deployment environments when developing Camunda 8 job workers -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. There is a significant difference in cross-runtime support. REST over HTTP/JSON is universally supported: every programming language has HTTP client libraries, every browser can make REST calls natively, and every serverless and edge runtime supports HTTP. gRPC requires specific client library support and has limitations in browser and certain serverless environments. For maximum deployment flexibility, REST provides broader compatibility than gRPC.

Use a custom Camunda-proprietary binary protocol optimised for job worker communication that provides better performance than both REST and gRPC -- this is invented. Camunda 8 offers two documented API protocols: the REST API over HTTP/JSON and the gRPC API using Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2. There is no third proprietary custom binary protocol. All official Camunda client libraries and SDKs use one of these two standard protocols. Teams choosing between REST and gRPC are choosing between broad compatibility and maximum performance, not between standard and proprietary options.

REST (HTTP/JSON) -- universally supported across languages, runtimes, and environments. Every HTTP-capable client (browsers, mobile apps, CLI tools, scripting environments, and all server-side languages) can call the Camunda REST API using standard HTTP libraries. gRPC is excellent for server-side high-throughput scenarios but requires gRPC-specific client libraries and has limited browser support without a proxy layer. For the broadest job worker compatibility across diverse runtime environments, REST is the appropriate choice. faster and stricter-typed but requires gRPC libraries / proto codegen, which isn't available everywhere (browsers especially require gRPC-Web). For broadest compatibility, REST wins.

a.text: Incorrect. REST's strength is universal compatibility: any HTTP client speaks it. Browsers (via fetch/XMLHttpRequest), mobile apps, CLI tools (curl), every server-side language. gRPC requires gRPC client libraries (typically auto-generated from .proto files) and isn't natively browser-compatible (needs gRPC-Web or a proxy). For broad reach, REST is the default. When to use gRPC instead: high-throughput scenarios (lower latency, binary encoding), bidirectional streaming, type-safety at compile time. For browser-facing and mobile-facing integrations, REST.

b.text: Incorrect. gRPC narrower in browser support.
